title: Chapter Seven: The Diagnosis
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D12+D7 -->
The elevator was a cage.

Not metaphorically—though it was that too—literal iron bars bolted to a platform that shuddered and screamed as it descended. Aria gripped the railing with both hands and watched the shaft walls slide upward: stone, then concrete, then rusted reinforcement bars exposed like bones in an open wound, then cables thick as her arm humming with tension overhead. The mechanism protested every meter of descent in a voice of grinding metal.

Nova stood across from her. Chrome arm braced against the bars, silver hair catching the thin light of a single LED strip bolted to the cage ceiling. Two Iron flanked the door—faces like closed fists, hands resting on weapons Aria didn't want to look at too closely. Nobody spoke. The descent took four minutes and felt like forty.
When the cage stopped, the gate opened onto noise.

Not sound. *Noise.* A wall of it—a full-body assault that hit Aria before she could brace for it. The clatter of machinery from a dozen directions. Hammers on anvils. The rhythmic thump of some vast compressor. Music—no, several musics—blaring from speakers mounted on crooked poles, bass and drums and voices tinny with distortion bleeding into each other in a war for airspace that nobody was winning. Someone was shouting. Several someones. A child crying. A saw cutting metal. All of it layered, overlapping, relentless.

The smell came next—copper thick on her tongue, machine oil and hot metal and something organic and sour, sweat and unwashed bodies and the chemical bite of solvents. She tasted it before she registered breathing it: a metallic tang that coated the inside of her mouth like a penny left under the tongue. Her eyes watered from the fumes, and when she wiped them, her hand came away streaked with grease from the elevator railing.
The air was warm. Moist. Close. It pressed against her skin like a damp palm.
And then she looked up and *saw* it.
Rust City.

A cavern so vast the far wall dissolved into haze—a space carved from the earth's gut and filled to bursting with everything the surface had chosen to forget. Buildings stacked three and four stories high, built from salvage and stubbornness: corrugated metal walls patched with plastic sheeting, balconies welded from pipe and rebar, walkways strung between structures on cables that swayed with foot traffic. Neon signs flickered and buzzed in colors the surface would never permit—vulgar pinks, aggressive greens, a blue so electric it looked like a scream. Salvaged LEDs ran in crooked lines along walkways and doorframes, a constellation of desperate illumination fighting the dark that pressed down from the cavern ceiling.

Forges blazed orange in the distance, their glow painting the underside of the cavern in heat. Steam vented from pipes. Sparks fountained from somewhere out of sight. People everywhere—walking fast, talking loud, carrying tools and crates and children. A woman with a welding torch balanced a baby on her hip. A man in spray-painted armor argued prices at a stall hung with scavenged electronics. Two kids chased each other through a crowd that parted around them like water around stones.

Above it all, the cavern ceiling vanished into darkness and infrastructure: massive ventilation shafts, their intakes humming; cable runs thick as tree trunks; what looked like the remains of a pre-Collapse industrial exhaust system, pocked and rusted but still breathing.

Aria's science brain kicked on like a generator snapping to life. She couldn't help it. The ventilation. The forges. The population density. The heat. She was already doing calculations she didn't want to do.
"Move," Nova said behind her, and a hand—flesh, not chrome—pushed between her shoulder blades.
The interrogation room was a supply closet with ambitions.

Four meters square, cut from raw stone, lit by a single LED panel that buzzed at a frequency just high enough to burrow into Aria's hindbrain. A metal table. Three chairs. A rack on the wall holding tools that were either for repair or interrogation—Aria chose not to examine them closely. The door was steel, dented, and closed with a sound like a coffin lid.

Nova sat across from her. The chrome arm rested on the table, light sliding along its articulated joints. The other two Iron had been dismissed. In their place stood a new figure: broad, restless, mid-thirties, with a shaved head and the kind of face that couldn't decide between friendly and feral. He was eating something from a waxed paper wrapper—some kind of pressed protein bar—and hadn't stopped since he entered.
"That's Mack," Nova said. "He doesn't stop eating. Don't take it personally."
"Don't stop talking either," Mack added, mouth full. "Fair warning."
Nova's attention returned to Aria with the precision of a blade finding its target. "You were in the surface settlement for what—three weeks?"
"Twenty-three days."
"Long enough to know their systems. Their vulnerabilities." Nova leaned forward. The chrome hand tapped the table once, a sharp metallic ping. "Long enough to be one of *them*."
"I wasn't one of them. I was a prisoner."
"They all say that."

"Iris Valerius poisoned the underground water supply with nightshade. I overheard the order. I was trying to escape when your people—" She stopped. Took a breath. The headache that had started in the elevator was sharpening, pressing behind her eyes like a thumb. "Your people captured me. During the raid."

Nova's expression didn't change. Not a flicker. But something in the set of her jaw shifted by a millimeter—the kind of tell Aria had learned to read in the underground, where a single wrong word could turn a conversation into a confrontation.
"Convenient story," Nova said.
"It's not a story. It's what happened."

"What I see is a surface-dweller with soft hands and clean clothes who just happened to be 'escaping' in the direction of my people's operation. What I see is someone Iris Valerius might have *positioned* for us to find."
Aria opened her mouth to argue—and stopped.

The headache wasn't stress. She knew stress headaches: a band of pressure across the temples, tight and dull. This was different. This was a throbbing concentration behind her forehead, accompanied by a nausea she'd been attributing to the elevator and the fumes. And the metallic taste in her mouth—it had gotten worse since the elevator. Not just copper and oil. Something sharper. Something that reminded her of the lab at MIT, of the exhaust hoods and the safety seminars and the chart on the wall that listed symptoms in descending order of urgency.

Her eyes moved to the ventilation grate in the corner of the room. Thin metal slats. Faint airflow. She inhaled deliberately—through her nose, slow, cataloging. Combustion byproducts. She could taste the partial oxidation signature. Carbon monoxide didn't have a smell, not technically, but it rode in on the same currents as carbon dioxide and aldehydes, and those she could detect: the acrid edge of incomplete combustion, the faintly sweet char.
"How long have people been complaining about headaches?" she asked.
Nova blinked. The non-sequitur landed like a stone in still water. "What?"

"Headaches. Morning nausea. Especially in the residential levels nearest the forge. Confusion in the elderly—maybe what looks like early dementia but isn't. People feeling like they're coming down with something that never quite becomes an illness." Aria pressed her fingers to her temples, the pressure a temporary relief. "How long?"
Mack stopped chewing. That was how Aria knew she'd hit something.

"Months," he said. "Maybe six. Ever since we rerouted the forge exhaust to the east corridor after the collapse." He looked at Nova. "People have been—Fara's grandmother. Dehlia. She's been foggy for weeks. And the morning sickness thing—Rin thought she was pregnant. Three times. Never was."

"Your vents are pulling combustion byproducts from the forge level into residential," Aria said. The words came fast and certain—the familiar rush of a diagnosis clicking into place, the same electric satisfaction she'd felt looking at the contaminated springs on the surface. Not pleasure. Something sharper. *Usefulness.* "Carbon monoxide. The forge exhaust is pressurizing the east corridor, and the intake for the residential ventilation is downstream. You're recirculating flue gas into living spaces. The symptoms match exactly—headache, nausea, confusion, especially in mornings when the forge ramps up and people are still in their quarters. CO binds to hemoglobin two hundred times more readily than oxygen. It accumulates overnight. Your people aren't sick. They're being *poisoned by their own air system.*"
Silence.
Not the conversational kind. The kind that happens when a room full of people realizes something they should have known and didn't.
Mack's protein wrapper crinkled in his still hand. Nova's chrome fingers had stopped tapping. Even the buzzing of the LED panel seemed to withdraw, as if the room itself was holding its breath.
"Show me," Nova said.
Aria found a piece of chalk in the supply drawers—white, half-used, probably originally meant for marking inventory. She turned to the far wall of the interrogation room and started drawing.

The diagram wasn't pretty. It didn't need to be. She sketched the cavern in cross-section: the forge level at the bottom, the east corridor above it, the residential quarter with its ventilation intakes. Arrows for airflow. Crosshatching for the collapsed section that had forced the exhaust reroute. She drew the problem first—forge exhaust pushing into the east corridor, the residential intake pulling that contaminated air into living spaces, the sick loop of poisoned circulation.
Then she drew the fix.

"Redirect the intake here." She chalked a new line. "Pull fresh air from the western shafts—they're upstream of the forge. Add a scrubbing chamber between the intake and the distribution manifold. You have activated charcoal from the forge—it's a byproduct of combustion, and I know you have it because I can smell the carbon profile. Pack it into a simple baffle system. The charcoal adsorbs CO and volatile organics." She drew the scrubber: a box, baffles, charcoal layers, airflow path. "Then create negative pressure in the forge exhaust—use the heat differential to drive a convection stack. The hotter exhaust rises, pulls fresh air through the forge, and vents out through the original shaft. The residential system never touches combustion byproducts again."

She stepped back. The diagram covered most of the wall—crude, chalky, alive with arrows and notations. RESTRUCTURE INTAKE. CHARCOAL SCRUBBER. NEGATIVE PRESSURE CONVECTION. Three elements. Simple. Elegant. Life-saving.
Nobody spoke.

Mack was staring at the wall like it had spoken to him in a language he'd forgotten he knew. His protein bar hung forgotten at his side. Nova's second-in-command—she'd heard someone call him Mack, but this was Mack *truly*, the one who never stopped talking—opened his mouth. Closed it. Opened it again.
"She just solved the thing three mechanics couldn't fix in six months."

The words landed in the room and broke something open. Not triumph—Aria didn't feel triumphant. She felt the headache still pulsing behind her eyes and the metallic taste still coating her tongue and the certainty that this was only the beginning of what was wrong here.

Nova stood slowly. She walked to the wall and studied the diagram, her chrome hand tracing the airflow arrows without touching them—hovering, the way a surgeon hovers over an X-ray. Her expression was unreadable. Then:
"Rust never sleeps." She said it like a proverb, like something her people said to explain the unending grind of survival underground. "But maybe it can be managed."
It wasn't gratitude. It wasn't trust. It was the first crack in a wall, and they both knew it.
"Implement this," Nova said to Mack. "Start with the intake reroute. I want the charcoal scrubber operational within forty-eight hours."

"On it." Mack was already moving, shoving the last of the protein bar into his mouth and heading for the door. He paused, looked back at Aria, and did something unexpected: he *smiled*. Not cautious. Not suspicious. Genuine. "You're weird as hell, surface girl. I think I like it."
He was gone before she could respond.
The headache didn't stop.

That bothered Aria more than anything else. If her diagnosis was correct—and she was certain it was—the CO exposure in this room should have been modest. Unpleasant but not acute. Yet the throbbing behind her eyes was *worsening*, and the metallic taste was deepening into something that went beyond combustion byproducts. Something that tasted like the springs on the surface. Like uranium decay chain isotopes.
Like the water Iris's people were drinking.
She sat back down across from Nova. The room felt smaller now, the walls pressing in with the weight of stone and certainty.
"I need to see your central power source," Aria said.
Nova's expression closed like a door slammed shut. "No."

"Your people have been breathing carbon monoxide for six months because no one understood the airflow dynamics. You fixed the vent. Good. But the CO isn't the only thing leaking, and you know it—or you suspect it, which is worse."
"I said no."

"The uranium isotopes in your surface water—they're coming from below. Not from the surface contamination. The decay signatures I measured in Iris's springs don't match surface-deposited radionuclides. They match *active subsurface fission*. Something down here is generating heat and neutrons and leaking radiation into the water table. Not CO. Not just CO. Something radioactive. And if it's leaking up into the surface springs, it's leaking *out* into whatever your power source is, which means—"
"Stop."
Nova's voice had changed. Not louder—quieter. The kind of quiet that comes before something breaks.
"—which means your power source is compromised. The containment is failing. And if it fails catastrophically, this whole cavern—"
"I said *stop*."

Nova stood. Her chair scraped the stone floor with a shriek that echoed off the walls. Her chrome hand was clenched, fingers digging into her palm hard enough that Aria could hear the servos whining. Her face was pale—pale in a way that the neon and the grease and the tough facade couldn't cover. This was fear. Real fear. The kind that came from knowing something and not being able to fix it and watching the clock run down.
"You know about the Heart." Not a question. The words came out flat, stripped of everything but the bare scaffolding of dread. "How do you know about the Heart?"

Aria felt the room tilt. The Heart. The name from Solon's fragments, from the pre-Collapse history he'd whispered in the dark moments before the raid. The power source that kept Rust City alive, that ran the forges and the lights and the ventilation that was slowly killing everyone. The thing at the center of everything.
"I don't know about it," Aria said carefully. "I know *of* it. I know it exists. And I know it's killing you."

Nova stared at her. The chrome hand unclenched. Reconclenched. The woman's silver hair caught the light of the buzzing LED, and for a moment—just a moment—she looked less like a leader and more like a woman standing at the edge of a cliff she'd known was there all along.
Then the mask came back. Harder this time. Reinforced.

"You're staying in the guest quarters," Nova said. "Not a request. Mack will station someone outside your door—for your protection." The echo of Iris's phrasing landed like a slap. "We'll talk about the Heart when I decide we'll talk about it. Not before."
She left. The steel door closed with the sound of a question being held underwater.

Aria sat in the buzzing room with her headache and her chalk diagram and the taste of copper and uranium and carbon monoxide coating her tongue, and she understood with the terrible clarity of a physicist watching a slope approach criticality that the Heart was not just a power source. It was a wound. And Rust City was built on top of it, bleeding slowly into the air and the water and the bones of everyone who called this place home.

The surface had its poisoned springs and its beautiful liar. The underground had its silent killer and its fierce, frightened warden. And somewhere between them, at the bottom of both worlds, something was failing.

She pressed her palm flat against the stone wall and felt the vibration—deep, steady, alive. The Heart. Beating beneath her feet. Begetting CO and isotopes and the slow accumulation of invisible harm that would, if no one intervened, kill every living thing in this cavern and the one above it.
She'd solved the ventilation. A charcoal filter and a rerouted intake. Simple.
The Heart was not going to be simple.

But Aria Chen had never met a system she didn't want to understand, and she'd never left a problem unsolved that she had the means to solve. Iris had called her interesting. Nova called her a surface-dweller. Mack called her weird as hell.
None of them had called her wrong.
She picked up the chalk and added a small notation to the corner of the diagram, below the arrows and the scrubber and the convection stack. Two words, barely visible in the buzzing half-light:
*The Heart.*
Then she sat down to wait, and to think, and to taste the radiation on her tongue and *calculate.*
